text: The surgeon divides the gastrohepatic ligament and identifies the right crus, continuing dissection into the hiatus towards the chest. The short gastric vessels on the superior fundus side are divided without fully mobilizing the greater curvature of the stomach. The left crus is identified, and the intrathoracic esophagus is dissected, revealing the diverticulum. Using blunt dissection and a vessel sealer, the diverticulum is circumferentially dissected. The esophagus is further mobilized into the chest, with care taken to protect the posterior vagus nerve.
An intraoperative upper GI endoscopy is performed, visually confirming the presence of the diverticulum above the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon uses a robotic 45mm green stapler to divide the fully mobilized intrathoracic diverticulum into the abdomen in two firings, ensuring the esophageal lumen is not impinged. The diverticulum is then removed using a 10mm bag.

The surgeon performs a lateral myotomy on the patient's right side, approximately ninety degrees away from the staple line, using Maryland bipolar forceps. The myotomy extends at least ten centimeters long and reaches well onto the stomach mucosa. An air leak test is conducted following the myotomy, which returns negative.

The surgeon performs a lateral fundoplication by approximating the fundus of the stomach to the left side of the esophagus, recreating the angle of His. A 2-0 suture is used to secure the fundoplication to the inferior staple line. An omental patch is then secured to the upper portion of the diverticulum staple line. The procedure concludes with a primary chiroplasty.
